articles.title: Machine Learning Approach Using MLP and SVM Algorithms for the Fault Prediction of a Centrifugal Pump in the Oil and Gas Industry

description_audit_fields.title: Machine Learning Approach Using MLP and SVM Algorithms for the Fault Prediction of a Centrifugal Pump in the Oil and Gas Industry
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: The article uses KNIME platform to implement and design the machine learning workflow for fault prediction of a centrifugal pump, describing workflow steps and nodes in text and figures, but does not provide downloadable workflow files, code, or input data URLs.

full_text_accessible.quote: This paper presents the preliminary development of a simple and easy to implement machine learning (ML) model for early fault prediction of a centrifugal pump in the oil and gas industry. ... To validate the learning capabilities of the ML model, two different algorithms—the Support Vector Machine (SVM) and the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)—are implemented in KNIME platform.
full_text_accessible.note: The article full text was accessible and provided detailed methodology and results.

uses_knime.quote: The open-source software KNIME platform is adopted to set-up the model workflow. The data used in this study come from a real oil and gas industry operating centrifugal pump inserted inside the production line of the SARLUX refinery (Sarroch, Italy).
uses_knime.article_section: method using data from actual working operating conditions is presented. The aim is to develop a
uses_knime.note: Direct statement that KNIME platform was used to set up the model workflow.

uses_knime.quote: Knime platform is used to design the workflow of centrifugal pump fault diagnosis, as reported in Figure 2.
uses_knime.note: Explicit mention of KNIME platform usage for workflow design.

provides_workflow_screenshots_or_figures.quote: Figure 2. Knime workflow built for fault diagnosis of a centrifugal pump.
provides_workflow_screenshots_or_figures.note: The article includes a figure showing the KNIME workflow.

describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.quote: Missing values are a common issue related to on-field operating machinery measurements... This has been performed by means of the “Missing Values” node in KNIME (Figure 2), using the linear interpolation technique... This step, achieved with the “Moving Average” node in KNIME, guarantees to take the correlation existing between a sample and the preceding ones of the time series into consideration and ensures noise attenuation.
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.note: Describes specific KNIME nodes used in the workflow for data processing.

describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.quote: MLP requires normalized data as input, z-score normalization has been made prior to the algorithm training by means of a “Normalizer” node. ... The algorithm implementation has been performed by means of the “RProp MLP” node.
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.note: Describes use of KNIME nodes for normalization and MLP training.

linked_resources.notes: No direct URLs or downloadable resources for input data, code, or workflows were provided in the article.

articles.title: Open-source QSAR models for pKa prediction using multiple machine learning approaches

description_audit_fields.title: Open-source QSAR models for pKa prediction using multiple machine learning approaches
description_audit_fields.knime_role_source_field: used KNIME for data curation and standardization in QSAR modeling
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: The article uses KNIME workflows for chemical structure curation and standardization in QSAR modeling but does not report KNIME version or provide downloadable workflows. Input data availability is reported but without direct URLs. Code availability is confirmed via GitHub link for OPERA implementation.

full_text_accessible.quote:
Mansouri et al. J Cheminform    (2019) 11:60
https://doi.org/10.1186/s13321-019-0384-1                                                         Journal of Cheminformatics
 RESEARCH ARTICLE                                                                                                                                Open Access
Open‑source QSAR models for pKa
prediction using multiple machine learning
approaches
Kamel Mansouri1*† , Neal F. Cariello1† , Alexandru Korotcov2, Valery Tkachenko2, Chris M. Grulke3 ,
Catherine S. Sprankle1 , David Allen1, Warren M. Casey4, Nicole C. Kleinstreuer4 and Antony J. Williams3
  Abstract
  Background: The logarithmic acid dissociation constant pKa reflects the ionization of a chemical, which affects
  lipophilicity, solubility, protein binding, and ability to pass through the plasma membrane. Thus, pKa affects chemical
  absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity properties. Multiple proprietary software packages exist
  for the prediction of pKa, but to the best of our knowledge no free and open-source programs exist for this purpose.
  Using a freely available data set and three machine learning approaches, we developed open-source models for pKa
  prediction.
full_text_accessible.note: The article full text is accessible as shown by the detailed extracted text lines from the abstract and other sections.

uses_knime.quote:
The initial dataset of 7912 chemical structures had 3614
acidic pKa values and 4298 basic pKa values. A KNIME
[34] workflow was used to standardize the structures and
generate QSAR-ready forms for modeling [13, 27, 30, 35].
This workflow excludes inorganic chemicals and mix-
tures; removes salts, solvents, and counterions; identifies
duplicates; and normalizes tautomers (e.g., nitro mesom-
ers and keto-enol forms, zwitterions are not modified).
This procedure yielded 6245 unique QSAR-ready struc-
tures.
uses_knime.note: The article explicitly states use of a KNIME workflow for chemical structure standardization and curation for QSAR modeling.

describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.quote:
A KNIME
[34] workflow was used to standardize the structures and
generate QSAR-ready forms for modeling [13, 27, 30, 35].
This workflow excludes inorganic chemicals and mix-
tures; removes salts, solvents, and counterions; identifies
duplicates; and normalizes tautomers (e.g., nitro mesom-
ers and keto-enol forms, zwitterions are not modified).
This procedure yielded 6245 unique QSAR-ready struc-
tures.
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.note: The article describes the KNIME workflow steps including exclusion of inorganics, removal of salts, duplicates identification, and tautomer normalization.

reports_input_data_availability.quote:
The experimental strongest acidic and strongest basic pKa values in water for 7912 chemicals were
  obtained from DataWarrior, a freely available software package.
reports_input_data_availability.note: The article reports input data availability from the freely available DataWarrior software package.

provides_code_or_scripts.quote:
The final kNN/SVM models were implemented in the
free and open source OPERA application (version 2.0)
that is available on Github at: https​://githu​b.com/NIEH
/OPERA​.
provides_code_or_scripts.note: The article provides code availability via the OPERA application on GitHub.

linked_resources.notes: Input data is reported as from DataWarrior software but no direct URL to data is provided. Code is available on GitHub for OPERA implementation. No downloadable KNIME workflows or workflow links are provided.

articles.title: The impact of engineering students' performance in the first three years on their graduation result using educational data mining

description_audit_fields.title: The impact of engineering students' performance in the first three years on their graduation result using educational data mining
description_audit_fields.knime_role_source_field: uses KNIME for predictive data mining model
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: The article uses KNIME as a platform for educational data mining predictive modeling but does not report KNIME version, downloadable workflows, input data URLs, code availability, or extension/plugin details.

full_text_accessible.quote: Education and Information Technologies (2019) 24:1527–1543 ... The academic performance of a student in a university is determined by a number of factors ... In this study, the relationship between the cognitive admission entry requirements and the academic performance of students in their first year, using their CGPA and class of degree was examined using six data mining algorithms in KNIME and Orange platforms.
full_text_accessible.note: The full article text was available for assessment as shown by multiple detailed excerpts.

uses_knime.quote: In this study, a data mining model was deployed on KNIME and Orange platform, and the veracity of the prediction was checked using regression analysis for performance comparison.
uses_knime.note: Explicit statement that KNIME was used for data mining modeling in the study.

provides_workflow_screenshots_or_figures.quote: The predictive KNIME based model deployed in this study is shown in Fig. 7.
provides_workflow_screenshots_or_figures.article_section: 6 The results of the predictive analysis using the KNIME platform
provides_workflow_screenshots_or_figures.note: Figure 7 shows the KNIME workflow, indicating inclusion of workflow figures.

describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.quote: The following features in the dataset were examined: the student’s entry age, the aggregate WAEC score, the JAMB score, the university based CUSAS score, the first-year grade classification while the actual CGPA was considered for the regression analysis. The KNIME and Orange Analytic applications were run on Core ™ i3-7100 U CPU 2.4GHz, 6 GB Ram computer running Windows 10 operating system.
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.note: The text describes the KNIME workflow steps and data features used in the analysis.

describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.quote: The predictive KNIME based model deployed in this study is shown in Fig. 7. The samples were selected using stratified sampling, and 70% of the data sample was used to train the model using 6 data mining algorithms; the Random Forest, the Tree Ensemble, the Decision Tree, the Naive Bayes, the Logistic Regression, and the Resilient back propagation (Rprop) Multi-Layer Perceptron algorithms.
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.article_section: 6 The results of the predictive analysis using the KNIME platform
describes_workflow_or_nodes_in_text.note: Description of the KNIME workflow steps and algorithms used.

linked_resources.notes: No direct URLs or links to input data, code, or downloadable KNIME workflows were provided in the article excerpts.

description_audit_fields.title: ImgLib2—generic image processing in Java
description_audit_fields.knime_role_source_field: mentions KNIME as underlying data model but does not use KNIME workflows
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: Article describes ImgLib2 Java library underlying the KNIME Image Processing toolbox but does not use KNIME workflows or report KNIME versions or workflow artifacts. Code is available via GitHub.

full_text_accessible.quote: ImgLib2 is an open-source Java library for n-dimensional data representation and manipulation with focus on image processing. ... It is the data model underlying ImageJ2, the KNIME Image Processing toolbox and an increasing number of Fiji-Plugins. Availability: ImgLib2 is licensed under BSD. Documentation and source code are available at http://imglib2.net and in a public repository at https://github.com/imagej/imglib.
full_text_accessible.note: Full article text excerpt was provided for assessment.

provides_code_or_scripts.quote: Availability: ImgLib2 is licensed under BSD. Documentation and source code are available at http://imglib2.net and in a public repository at https://github.com/imagej/imglib.
provides_code_or_scripts.note: The article provides source code availability information and repository URL.

linked_resources.notes: Code repository and software homepage URLs are provided; no workflow or input data URLs.

articles.title: Heart disease classification using data mining tools and machine learning techniques

description_audit_fields.title: Heart disease classification using data mining tools and machine learning techniques
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: The article uses KNIME 4.0.2 as one of six data mining tools to perform heart disease classification analysis. It reports the KNIME version and discusses KNIME usability and workflow design but does not provide downloadable workflows or code. Input data availability is reported via UCI repository mention without direct URL. Extension/plugin dependencies are not reported, but installation source URL for KNIME is provided.

full_text_accessible.quote:
Health and Technology (2020) 10:1137–1144
https://doi.org/10.1007/s12553-020-00438-1
    ORIGINAL PAPER
Heart disease classification using data mining tools and machine
learning techniques
Ilias Tougui 1                            1
                   & Abdelilah Jilbab & Jamal El Mhamdi
                                                                   1
Received: 14 February 2020 / Accepted: 11 May 2020 / Published online
# IUPESM and Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 20
full_text_accessible.note: The article full text was accessible for assessment as shown by the extracted text lines from the abstract and metadata.

uses_knime.quote: Six data mining tools were used in this research: Orange, Weka, RapidMiner, Knime, Matlab, and Scikit-learn, and six machine learning techniques were applied on the dataset using each one of the tools: Logistic regression, Support Vector Machine, K Nearest Neighbors, Artificial Neural Network, Naïve Bayes, and, Random Forest.
uses_knime.note: The article explicitly states that KNIME was used as one of six data mining tools in the study.

uses_knime.quote: For advanced users, we strongly recommend using Knime or RapidMiner, both platforms/tools require prior knowledge and understanding of machine learning techniques, and data mining procedures. They are powerful in terms of data manipulation, creating complex workflows, parameter tuning, and they allow a total control of algorithms.
uses_knime.note: The article discusses KNIME as a recommended tool for advanced users, indicating usage and evaluation.

reports_knime_version.quote:
Knime        4.0.2  Open source GUI – visual            Workflow
                      GNU           program-
                                    ming

Availability of data and material UCI machine learning repository.
School of Information and Computer Science, University of California,
Irvine. https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/datasets/Heart+Disease

&        Matlab: https://www.mathworks.com/downloads/
&        Scikit-learn: https://scikit-learn.org/stable/
reports_input_data_availability.article_section: results. Furthermore, we made several recommendations
reports_input_data_availability.note: The article reports input data availability by referencing the UCI machine learning repository and provides URLs for data mining tools including KNIME.

articles.title: Latest Tools for Data Mining and Machine Learning
articles.venue: International Journal of Innovative Technology and Exploring Engineering

description_audit_fields.title: Latest Tools for Data Mining and Machine Learning
description_audit_fields.venue: International Journal of Innovative Technology and Exploring Engineering
description_audit_fields.knime_role_source_field: Mentions KNIME as a data mining tool but does not use it in a study
description_audit_fields.evidence_notes: Article is a review listing KNIME among other data mining tools without using KNIME in a concrete study or workflow.

full_text_accessible.quote: Many open source and licensed tools like Weka, RapidMiner, KNIME, and Orange are available for Data Mining and predictive analysis. This paper discusses about different to available for Data Mining and Machine Learning, followed by the description, pros and cons of these tools.
full_text_accessible.note: The article full text was accessible and includes mention of KNIME among data mining tools.

linked_resources.notes: No downloadable KNIME workflows, input data URLs, or code repositories were reported or linked.
